Long ago, before the sun learned to sleep, the sky was a ceaseless, blinding white. The people of the Earth grew weary, for their shadows were thin and their dreams had no place to hide. They pleaded with the Great Weaver, spider-god of the firmament, to craft a blanket for the world.

The Weaver agreed but demanded a price: the silence of the ocean. The waves, proud and boisterous, refused to hush. So, the Weaver spun a net not of thread, but of stolen whispers. He cast it wide, catching the sun in a snare of twilight. As the light struggled, it bled colors—violet, indigo, and burning orange—staining the white sky.

To cool the sun’s anger, the Weaver plucked the silver scales from the ocean’s surface while it slept, pinning them against the darkening fabric. These became the stars. Finally, he took the ocean’s own reflection, a pale and quiet disc, and hung it as the moon to watch over the tides. Now, the sun is allowed to return only when the Weaver rests, but every evening the net is cast again, reminding the ocean that even the loudest voice must eventually give way to the peace of the night.

One day, a young stone carver named Caelum climbed the mountain to find the perfect stone for a statue. Hearing Eos’s mournful tune, he began to hum along. Delighted, Eos descended, invisible but present, and whispered her harmonies into his ear. Caelum, thinking the mountain itself was singing, shouted, "Who is there?"

Eos, not knowing human speech, simply mimicked his tone, shouting back, "Who is there?"

Frustrated, Caelum yelled, "Show yourself!" and Eos, thinking this was a game, repeated, "Show yourself!"

Believing he was being mocked by a demon, Caelum fled down the mountain, never to return. Heartbroken, Eos fractured her spirit into thousands of pieces, embedding herself into the rocks and caves of the world. Now, whenever a lonely traveler calls out in a desolate place, Eos answers, forever hoping to finish the duet she started so long ago.
